items.name: The Pin & Sprint
items.why_it_works: The target pins both centre backs and wins or holds the first ball. Because the centre backs are occupied and drawn toward him, the space behind them belongs to the runner. Neither works alone: a target with no runner just recycles, and a runner with no pin gets caught offside against a comfortable line.
items.exemplar: Giroud + Mbappé (France 2018, Giroud scored zero and made Mbappé's tournament possible).

items.name: The Inverted Pair
items.why_it_works: The winger wants to come inside onto his strong foot; that only stays viable if someone supplies the width behind him, forcing the opposition fullback into a two-man problem every possession.

items.name: The Stretch & Slip
items.why_it_works: The mirror image of the Inverted Pair: the winger holds maximum width and pins wide, opening the inside channel for a runner from deep.
items.exemplar: Coman wide + Goretzka underlapping (Bayern 19/20); Di María wide + Khedira arriving (Madrid).

items.name: The Vacuum
items.why_it_works: The false nine drops and drags a centre back, or forces a free-man concession. The space he vacates is attacked diagonally by the wide players: the goals go to the wingers, the system goes through the striker.

items.name: The Feeder & Finisher
items.why_it_works: The ten lives between the lines facing goal. The runner's constant threat in behind is what forces the opposition line deep enough to give the ten that space, and the ten's through-balls are what make the runs pay.

items.name: The Magnet & Ghost
items.why_it_works: The nine occupies defenders and finishes; the Raumdeuter reads where the resulting attention leaves holes and arrives unmarked.

items.name: Big-Little Strike Partnership
items.why_it_works: The 4-4-2 classic: the target wins and sets, the second striker plays off his knockdowns and shoulders. The original strike-partnership synergy in football.

items.name: The Brain & Shield
items.why_it_works: One pivot player faces forward and distributes, the other wins the ball and covers the other's risk. Two controllers together go soft without the ball unless control is the identity itself; two destroyers together go sterile with it.
items.exemplar: Alonso + Khedira (Madrid); Kimmich + Goretzka (Bayern, controller plus box-crasher variant).

items.name: The Anchor & Arrows
items.why_it_works: The pivot's positional discipline licenses the eights to attack the half-spaces, and the eights' movement is what gives the pivot forward passing lanes.

items.name: The Quarterback Link
items.why_it_works: A pivot with elite long range turns every regain into a launch: one pass from deep finds the wide runners' channels before the opponent resets.
items.exemplar: Alonso to Ronaldo/Di María; Alexander-Arnold's diagonals to Mané (fullback variant).

items.name: The Engine & Insurance
items.why_it_works: The high-work-rate eight can crash the box and press aggressively because the anchor stays home. Universal across formations.

items.name: The Aggressor & Sweeper
items.why_it_works: One centre back steps out to kill attacks early; the other defends the space that stepping creates. Two stoppers together leave holes behind; two coverers together put no pressure on the ball. Universal.
items.exemplar: Ramos (front foot) + a covering partner; van Dijk covering an aggressive partner.

items.name: The High-Line Contract
items.why_it_works: A high line is only playable if the space behind it is defended by someone, either the keeper sweeping or a centre back or fullback winning footraces. Required by the Positional Possession and Gegenpress identities; the fit engine should warn strongly when a high-block identity lacks this unit.

items.name: The Build-Out Spine
items.why_it_works: The players who beat the first press line between them. Required by the Positional Possession identity.

items.name: The Rest-Defence Flank
items.why_it_works: The inverted fullback adds a counterpress body centrally, the winger keeps width, and the centre back covers the vacated flank channel. Home: possession identities.

items.name: Flank Balance
items.why_it_works: A winger who does not track back must have a fullback who does not bomb on. Asymmetry across the two flanks is fine; double exposure on the same flank is not.
items.exemplar: France 2018's right side (Pavard staying home behind Mbappé); the inverse is Matuidi's tucked-in role on the French left.
